Managing Tech: 1:1

Mar 23, 2026 · 18 min read

Enablement

This is part of a series:

  • Managing Tech: People
  • Managing Tech: Heroes
  • Managing Tech: Professionals
  • Managing Tech: Time
  • Managing Tech: Cadence
  • Managing Tech: 1:1 (you are here)

If you read the post in this series about meeting cadence, and felt overwhelmed, I am here to tell you that I get it - it is a lot. In this post I will dial it back a bit and return focus to the people. As I have said before, without the people, there is no manager. Making people heroes should be a primary goal of your leadship style. Let us do that then - focus on the people to make them heroes. How do we achieve that? It all starts with the 1:1.

What Is a 1:1?

The 1:1 meeting is exactly what it sounds like - a one-on-one meeting between you and one of your direct reports. On the surface, this sounds easy. You setup and meeting, show up, and talk about what is going on at work. Congratulations, you are half-way there. You are also half-way off the rails already. If you show up just to talk about work - the business - then you are missing the point of the meeting in the first place, the person.

One-on-one meetings should be people focused, not operationally focused.

The First 1:1

When I get a new team, or a new team member, my first one-on-one with them is a little different than my one-on-one meetings will be after that initial visit. At this point, we do not even know one another yet. It would be presumptuous of me to launch into operational behaviors. You only get one chance to make that first impression. You can never have your first one-on-meeting again. I know very little about that person, and they know very little about me. I might have some hand-off notes from their previous manager, but those will come with bias. The direct report may have heard about me from others, but again, bias and hearsay rule their perception. The place to start then is to get to know one another.

When scheduling the first one-on-one meeting, be sure to include a general outline of what you will be talking about - and as a general rule, never send a blank meeting invite. In this case that will be: introductions, what is working, what is not working, what they want to get from their employment, and their personal contact preferences.

Introductions

I generally start my first one-on-one meeting with introductions. To remove social pressures, and to give the person time to consider how they want to introduce themselves, I will generally start this conversation. It will give the direct report a pattern to follow. I begin with my broader industry experience - how I got here. Then I turn to my experience within the company (if any). From there I talk about my leadership style and a little about my expectations. I then close with a few personal notes about myself - where I live, why I chose to live there, my family, a book I am reading, or a movie a recently saw, etc.

Then it is their turn. For you, this differs slightly from a general conversation in that you want to take notes. The facts that the individual chooses to surface about themselves are important details. They are important details for the person, so they should be important details for you. When ideas are important, you write them down. This is how they present themselves - the narrative of their life with regards to work. The only bias is their own, and that is the bias you want.

Let us say that you hear that a direct report is into Star Trek. Knowing this, and capturing it for future reference, could potentially help you form analogies to coach the person in the future. Imagine a dialog where you say something like "You know when Captain Picard did..."

What Is Working?

Now that you both know a little about each other, you can start to build out a larger narrative. I like to be blunt here and simply ask what they think is working in the business today. This gives the direct report a chance to express positive thoughts and emotions. It also helps to set the stage for an authentic relationship moving forward. Again, you should be taking notes. These are cues to help you understand what not to mess with in your first 30 days.

If this is a new employee, you can still ask this question. You can ask about the hiring and onboarding process - important notes for broader hiring and team building optimizations for the future. You can also ask about what they liked at previous employers. It is okay - we have all had previous employment. If they liked something about a past employer, you want to know what that was. Maybe there are new ideas to be had there to help improve your own business.

What Is Not Working?

Now comes what is likely the hardest pivot of the conversation - what is broken. Depending on the seniority of the direct report, you will get various levels of depth as a response. Again, take notes. These are your own personal action items for the future. If a direct report is coming into the office day after day, and is grumbling under their breath about how silly or painful it is that they have to follow a certain process, but they are not telling you, then you will miss the first opportunity to help them be heroes. Those processes may be present for good reason, in which case it is your job to go out, research them, and report back to the employee. If those processes are indeed broken, then you can start charting a path towards a fix, and communicate those plans back to the employee.

To be clear, and in no uncertain terms, what you are hearing here is "This is how you can make me a hero." Do not squander that opportunity.

If this is a new employee, you can still ask this question. Chances are that the employee left their previous role for a reason. You want to understand those reasons so you can avoid them in the future if at all possible. What brought them to your employer? To your team?

Contact Preferences

Not everybody likes to be contacted in the same manner. Some prefer that you schedule a meeting. Some prefer email. Some prefer corporate chat channels. Take this time to understand the employees preferences. You also likely have preferences, and you should communicate them here as well. I will, for example, let the employee know that regardless of how they contact me, that they can expect some response within 24 hours. It may not be a response with an answer, but it will be a response. Likewise, my expectation is that whatever communication channel they prefer, that I expect a similar 24 hour response. This baseline shared understanding of how we communicate is critical.

Your direct reports know how they work best - what times they will be most productive, and under what circumstances. You want your team to be productive. If a direct report suggests that they do their business housekeeping on Monday afternoons, then that is when you want to contact them. Dropping an email or chat with an urgent notice in the middle of their productive time will end that productivity.

Since this is your first one-on-one with the employee, you probably scheduled this meeting for what you thought was the best time possible. Or maybe you scheduled the meeting for when it worked best for you (that is okay at this point). Close the meeting by asking what time the direct report would like to meet for their weekly one-on-one moving forward. Schedule it right there, with them, on the call.

A Word on Notes

You should always take notes in your one-on-one meetings. Those notes should be in a shared document with the direct report. In this manner, the employee can see how you are interpreting their communication. A link to this shared document should then go into the meeting invitation for your regularly scheduled one-on-one meetings. When you encounter topics that you need to address with the employee throughout the week, be sure to put those notes into that same shared document. Now your direct report knows beforehand, in an asynchronous fashion, what it is that will be discussed in their next one-on-one meeting. They will have a chance to process those thoughts before the meeting, and come to the meeting ready for those specific conversations.

This shared document approach also gives both you and your direct report a clear history and progression. Nothing is left to the unknown. Transparency is key to building trust - not surprises. When performance reviews come around, you both now have a complete history to reference. In the unfortunate case that the employee needs performance coaching, you also now have a history to reference. These notes should never be shared directly with anybody but the direct report. Of course you can pull from them as needed summarily, but these notes should be confidential in nature.

The Heart of the 1:1

Before going deeper into the one-on-one meeting, it is important to have the right mindset. To that end, it is important to remember that 1:1 time is employee time. While you will most certainly have passdown notes, this time should otherwise be focused on the direct report. Team meetings are the place for broader dissemination of information from you to your broader team.

It is likely that the template you use for your 1:1 meetings will change over time. As a starting template however, I suggest the following areas of focus.

  • Wellness: How are you doing?
  • Readiness: Do you have everything you need to be successful?
  • Obstacles: What is standing in your way?
  • Recognition: What have you accomplished that you feel good about?
  • Feedback: How am I doing as your manager?

Let us break these down with a few more specifics.

Wellness

I like to think of this area as work/life balance. While it should be clear that there is no need to have employees specify details about their personal lives, giving somebody the opportunity to share can help you make them a hero. If somebody is being abused at home (yes, I have had this exchange), then it is likely that your employer has partnerships in place to help. Employees may not be aware of these programs. You are not human resources. You are not there to fix these serious problems - that is likely beyond your training and experience. You are there to listen, and you can take action when somebody's well-being is threatened.

Likewise, back on the work front, you want to make sure that your direct reports feel physically and psychologically safe. Not everybody shows up in meetings in the same manner. Perhaps this specific direct report has feedback they want to share, but others in the team dominate the conversation. Does this direct report want you to make space for their communication style? Perhaps they feel singled out by being asked directly? There is no stupid question. As a manager, you need to ensure that any inquiry be treated with respect, and that nobody is humiliated, ignored, blamed, or punished for asking.

Is your direct employee burning the proverbial midnight oil just to keep afloat? Are there process you have learned that you can put in place to help? While different cultures treat this differently, I believe that employees should work to live, not live to work. If somebody on your team is burning out, then the 1:1 is the place to catch it, and to course correct as necessary.

Yes, there will be times when we are all asked to put in a few extra hours. The ebb and flow of your business will likely dictate those times. You must be sure to allow for the balance to return by emphasizing that is totally acceptable for people to take the time they need, when they need it.

Readiness

I generally like to have 1 - 3 personal goals for each specific direct report. These can then be reviewed in the 1:1 meeting at a regular cadence. Sometimes you might find that a goal was too large and needs to be broken down. Sometimes you might find that a goal was not a good fit for the employee, and that you need remove it. That is fine.

I once had an employee that would save up all their personal time, and take the entirety of December off. When they returned in January, they had a specific list of objectives (usually around 8 - 10) that they wanted to accomplish in the new year. This approach is fine, too, so long as personal goals are present, noted, and reviewed.

These goals should take considerable conversation. You want the goals to line up with where the employee wants to go with their career, and to fit the needs of the business at the same time. Finding that harmony takes time.

Consider the SMART framework. Specific, measured, actionable, relevant, and time-bound.

Great! Now everybody has goals. Do they also have what they need to be successful in reaching those goals? Goals aside, do they have everything they need to be successful with their day-to-day work? This is an opportunity for you to dig deeper and find ways that you can help your direct reports to be heroes. Perhaps they need more knowledge in a given area. Can you pair them with a senior? Are there courses offered by your employer to help flesh out that missing knowledge? Are there groups inside the company that might help cater to solving a direct reports specific challenges (not problems, challenges)? Maybe they need a mentor that you can help locate and arrange? Maybe you need to schedule time to share your own knowledge?

Obstacles

Where readiness is more of a broad view, obstacles zero in on the here and now. Maybe the direct report is struggling to get feedback from another team in a timely manner. This is a cue for you to step in. You can track that team or person down, and have a conversation to understand where the gaps may be. You, as the leader, should find ways to bridge those gaps.

If there are specific challenges being faced by the direct report that are slowing them down, then you want to know. If there are changes to process that could be made that would increase productivity, you also want to know. Put simply - these are action items for you to record and follow up on to ensure resolution. That resolution may not always go the way you or your direct report expected, but addressing them helps everybody. As the old adage goes "A rising tide lifts all boats."

Recognition

After all the depth and headiness of readiness and obstacles, this is my favorite part of 1:1 conversations. What is something that your direct report feels good about accomplishing over the last week (or whatever the interval is between 1:1 meetings)? Openly celebrate these wins with your direct report!

Perhaps the employee has no significant "win" for the week. Take this opportunity to share what you feel your employee is doing well.

No achievement is too small - and achievements due recognition do not have to stem from the workplace. Perhaps your direct report has been training for their first marathon, and have hit a new milestone? Perhaps they have been meaning to get their car repaired (or replaced), and they finally took the time to make that happen? Maybe you can encourage them to take the time? Perhaps they completed a book, or a puzzle, or saw that movie that they have been wanting to see? There is far too little praise in the world - take time to end that drought.

Wins shared in 1:1 meetings need to stay in 1:1 meetings. Just because an accomplishment is shared with you during the 1:1 does not give you permission to share that elsewhere, or with the broader team. Again, confidentiality is key. If you really want to share more broadly - say in the manager meetings - then ask for permission first.

Feedback

Oh, yes, this is uncomfortable at first, but you need to know - how are you doing as a manager? You spend all this time talking with the employee about how they are/not doing, it is only fair that you give the employee time to share their critique of you. Feedback should be ongoing in both directions. When the annual performance review comes around, there should be no surprises. One per year feedback can be psychologically devastating, and are usually timed to the end-of-year holidays. That is just bad news all the way around.

Taking feedback as a manager takes practice and patience - even if the feedback is positive. Do not run away from your best opportunity to improve. If you do, it will show up later in ways that can lead to dysfunction for you, your team, and your management.

Tuning the 1:1

While these are all good starting points, you may, in time, find that you want to tune the one-on-one meeting to your business and style. Go for it! During my time at Amazon, these basic principles evolved to have more structure that was pertinent to the conversations I was having with other teams and my own management. That outline looked something like the following (many details omitted).

  • Previous annual performance
  • Target annual performance
  • Last quarterly growth conversation
  • Alignment with corporate objectives (training, mandates)
  • Last time off, next time off, and time off balance
  • Self-identified goals and corresponding progress
  • Progress from the previous week
  • Priorities for the week ahead
  • Problems, blockers, obstacles
  • Feedback for me as a manager

Improving the 1:1

I have repeatedly emphasized taking notes in a shared document for 1:1 meetings. I have also mentioned that the 1:1 is the direct reports time. They should then in turn have the power to cancel or reschedule those meetings as needed - and you should let them know that they can do that as needed.

Also worth considering then is how you approach the 1:1 meeting. If this were your time, how would you want it to be treated? Would you want your manager to show up unprepared? You need to make sure that you are operating from the 1:1 notes as a source of truth for the direct report.

To that end, do your homework before the meeting. Review that source of truth prior to the scheduled 1:1 meeting. Have a viewpoint going into the meeting. As an example, what is the time off balance for the employee? Should you encourage them to take some time off? Are there corporate benchmarks such as internal training that the employee has not yet taken? Is the direct report hitting an employment milestone such as one year at the company? You should know the details around these areas before arriving at the meeting - even better if you put them in the notes prior to the meeting.

I generally schedule 1:1 meetings to last 30-minutes, but they regularly go longer. I prepare for that by leaving 30 more minutes between my 1:1 meetings and any other meetings. Nothing says you are not important like suddenly having to leave because you managed your time poorly.

Next Steps

The one-on-one meeting is probably your most important meeting of the week. In many cases, the conversations I have with my direct reports leads to me fleshing out the week ahead with action items of my own beyond the day-to-day work already scheduled. This is a good thing - it is the reason your role exists. It is your key to unlocking how to make your employees heroes for what it is that they are trying to achieve with their careers. Take the time. Make the time. Be prepared for the meeting. Take [shared] notes that encourage trust and open channels of communication.

Let's Build Something Together

Have a project, collaboration, or opportunity you would like to discuss? I am especially interested in work involving web architecture, data, IoT, and developer experience.

Prefer email? You can reach me at kevin@ketnerlake.com.
